title: A Nation on Paper
bullets: In July 1776 men in a hot Philadelphia hall voted to start a country that did not yet exist, fighting a war it was losing, against the most powerful empire on earth, on the strength of an argument written on paper.

The thirteen colonies were not a unit: New England Puritans, the mixed middle colonies with Scots-Irish settlers, and a southern world of plantations worked by enslaved Africans were as foreign to each other as a Virginia planter and a Boston merchant.

The grievance began after 1763 when Britain, deep in debt from the Seven Years' War, taxed the colonies through the Stamp Act and Townshend duties, and the colonists answered with the constitutional slogan 'no taxation without representation.'
Thomas Paine's Common Sense, published January 1776, sold over 100,000 copies in a population of 2.5 million and shifted public opinion from reconciliation toward independence.
Thomas Jefferson, a thirty-three-year-old Virginia planter, drafted the Declaration of Independence and wrote the sentence the whole book orbits: 'all men are created equal.'

The man who wrote that sentence owned more than one hundred human beings and kept owning and selling them for the rest of his life, a contradiction the Congress deepened by striking Jefferson's passage blaming the king for the slave trade.

Educated founders expected the experiment to fail because every large republic in history had rotted into tyranny, and Montesquieu's accepted theory held a republic could only survive in a small territory.

The bet was to build a republic at a scale no republic had survived at, in a society that did not agree it was one society, and most informed observers in Europe expected it to come apart within a generation.

title: Winning the War
bullets: Britain should have won easily, holding the most powerful navy on earth, a professional army, and global wealth, against thirteen quarrelsome governments with no navy, no national treasury, and a large Loyalist population.
George Washington was not a brilliant tactician and lost many battles, but his genius was keeping the Continental Army in existence, because as long as the army survived the rebellion was not over.
The war's true hinge was Saratoga in October 1777, where an entire British army of around 6,000 men under General Burgoyne surrendered, the proof France wanted that the Americans could win.
In 1778 France signed a formal alliance with the United States, converting a colonial rebellion into a global war and committing its navy, money, and eventually a land army.
The Marquis de Lafayette paid his own way to America at barely twenty, served without pay, and became so close to the childless Washington that the two treated each other as father and son.
At Valley Forge in the winter of 1777 to 1778, Baron von Steuben drilled the army until it could form a battle line and maneuver as a unit instead of an armed crowd.
The southern campaign turned partly on the enslaved: tens of thousands fled to British lines on the promise of freedom, the largest emancipation in North America before the Civil War.

At Yorktown in October 1781 a French fleet shut the sea door and Washington's and Rochambeau's armies shut the land door, trapping Cornwallis, who surrendered roughly eight thousand men and broke Britain's will to continue.

title: Winning the Peace
bullets: The American negotiators Franklin, Adams, and Jay quietly disobeyed Congress's instructions, opened separate talks with Britain behind France's back, and gambled their reputations on a better deal alone.
The Treaty of Paris of 1783 recognized full American independence and set the western boundary at the Mississippi River, roughly doubling the territory Americans thought they had been fighting for.

Somewhere between sixty thousand and a hundred thousand Loyalists left the United States permanently, one of the largest forced political exoduses of the eighteenth century, defining the new nation partly by who it drove out.
Pennsylvania passed the first gradual abolition law in 1780, freeing no one already enslaved but declaring children of enslaved mothers free only after serving the owner until age twenty-eight.

For Native nations the Revolution was a catastrophe: in 1779 Washington ordered the Sullivan campaign to burn roughly forty Iroquois towns, orchards, and food stores, and the Iroquois named him 'Town Destroyer.'
In the 1783 Newburgh Conspiracy, unpaid officers hinted at marching on Congress, and Washington walked in unannounced, fumbled for his spectacles, and talked the mutiny down without a shot.

At the end of 1783 Washington surrendered his commission and went home, giving up power he could almost certainly have kept, an act with no real parallel among victorious generals from Caesar to Cromwell to Napoleon.

title: The Confederation That Couldn't
bullets: The Articles of Confederation, ratified in 1781, were a treaty of friendship among thirteen small countries: no president, no national courts, one vote per state, and amendment requiring unanimous consent.
The weakness was deliberate, designed by men terrified of recreating the distant, coercive central power they had just overthrown, but the cure for tyranny turned out to be a recipe for impotence.
The Land Ordinance of 1785 laid the western grid of six-mile townships still visible from the air, reserving one section per township to fund public schools.
The Northwest Ordinance of 1787 created a template by which territories became fully equal states and banned slavery in the Northwest Territory, drawing the first federal line around slavery.
The single fatal flaw was that Congress could not tax: it could only ask the states for money, the states mostly did not pay, and American credit was destroyed.
American sailors seized by the Barbary states sat in North African prisons because the country could neither pay tribute nor build a navy, and John Adams was received coldly in London.

In 1786 to 1787 farmers in western Massachusetts, many of them war veterans, shut down courts and marched on a federal arsenal in Shays' Rebellion, put down not by the United States but by a privately funded militia.

Shays' Rebellion convinced men like Washington the republic was sliding toward the anarchy-then-tyranny death classical theory predicted, and it is why the Philadelphia meeting of 1787 was packed and urgent.

title: The Constitution
bullets: In the summer of 1787 fifty-five men met in the building where independence had been declared, closed the windows in the heat, swore themselves to secrecy, and wrote an entirely new framework with no legal authority.
James Madison arrived best prepared with the Virginia Plan and the radical theory that a large republic is safer than a small one because so many competing factions check each other.
Small states countered with the New Jersey Plan keeping one equal vote per state, and the Convention deadlocked for weeks and nearly collapsed over representation.
The Great Compromise split Congress into a House apportioned by population and a Senate with two seats per state, using bicameralism to balance states against each other.
They invented a single elected President serving a fixed four-year term, commander of the military but unable to declare war, every power fenced by a counter-power held somewhere else.

The Three-Fifths Clause counted each enslaved person as three-fifths for apportionment, giving slaveholding states extra seats and electoral votes in proportion to the human beings they held in bondage.
The slave-trade clause shielded the Atlantic trade from federal interference until 1808, and the fugitive-slave clause bound free states to return escaped people, nationalizing slavery's reach.
The framers chose union over confronting slavery and did it knowingly, never writing the word 'slavery' while building it into the structure, leaving the question for a later generation.

title: Ratification
bullets: The Constitution went to specially elected ratifying conventions, not state legislatures, and would take effect once nine of thirteen states ratified, a deliberate and strictly illegal break from the Articles' unanimity rule.
Supporters called themselves Federalists, a reassuring name, while opponents got stuck with the negative label Anti-Federalists and never recovered the messaging ground.

The Anti-Federalists' core argument was the orthodox one, that a large republic with a strong center would slide toward tyranny, and they made the devastating point that the document had no bill of rights.

Hamilton, Madison, and Jay wrote eighty-five Federalist essays as 'Publius,' campaign documents to flip doubtful ratifying votes that remain the deepest explanation of how the machine was meant to work.
Federalist No. 51 laid out the engineering principle that 'ambition must be made to counteract ambition,' because 'if men were angels, no government would be necessary.'
Virginia ratified narrowly 89 to 79 after Madison debated Patrick Henry, and New York followed by an even tighter 30 to 27 because being left outside the union alone was untenable.
The promised Bill of Rights, ratified by 1791 as the first ten amendments, was the negotiated price the Anti-Federalists extracted, making American constitutional liberty a thing the losers won.

Ratification decided the United States would be a single federal republic with a strong national government, but it did not decide slavery, which went in unaltered inside the structure's load-bearing wall.

title: Inventing the Presidency
bullets: On April 30, 1789, George Washington took the oath in New York City for a job no human being had ever held, knowing every single thing he did would become a precedent.
The Electoral College chose Washington unanimously, the only unanimous presidential election in American history, because he was the one figure every faction trusted not to become a king.
Washington let the plain title 'Mr. President' win over grander proposals, because a republican chief magistrate addressed like a monarch starts becoming one.

The Constitution did not mention a cabinet; Washington created one by appointing and regularly consulting Jefferson, Hamilton, Knox, and Randolph, an institution conjured out of practice rather than text.
He served two terms and deliberately declined a third, establishing a two-term limit that held as an unwritten rule for nearly a century and a half.

The First Federal Congress did work so foundational it has been called a second constitutional convention, creating the executive departments and passing revenue laws that gave the government an actual income.

The Judiciary Act of 1789 built a whole branch the Constitution had barely sketched, setting the Supreme Court's size and creating lower federal courts, with one provision later struck down in the case that invented judicial review.
A president, a cabinet, a court system, and a working treasury were all invented in real time by men who often disagreed bitterly, and the astonishing thing was that it ran at all.

title: Hamilton's Revolution
bullets: Alexander Hamilton, the immigrant orphan running the Treasury, faced a United States that owed money it could not pay to foreign governments, domestic creditors, and the separately indebted states.
His Report on Public Credit proposed funding the debt at par, paying old IOUs at full face value, on the reasoning that a government that always pays can always borrow cheaply in the next emergency.

Assumption, the federal takeover of state war debts, was deliberately a nation-binding device disguised as accounting, attaching a wealthy creditor class's self-interest to the national government's survival.
Jefferson and Madison saw the whole program as a betrayal of the Revolution and a different idea of America, agrarian and decentralized against Hamilton's commercial, industrial, central vision.

At a single private dinner hosted by Jefferson in 1790, the deadlock broke: assumption would pass and the permanent capital would go to the Potomac, the financial system and the capital traded over a meal.
The Bank of the United States, chartered 1791, provoked the first great fight over the limits of federal power, Jefferson's strict construction against Hamilton's doctrine of implied powers.
The Report on Manufactures argued the government should build American industry from scratch with protective tariffs and subsidies; Congress declined most of it but it became Clay's later blueprint.

By the early 1790s the founders had sorted into Federalists and Democratic-Republicans, parties the framers had not wanted and the Constitution does not mention, which formed anyway because the argument was real.

title: The World at War
bullets: The French Revolution split American politics along the party line: Democratic-Republicans saw an embattled sister republic, Federalists saw bloody anarchy and championed orderly Britain.
In 1793 the French minister Citizen Genêt recruited Americans into privateering ventures on American soil, an open challenge to whether the United States controlled its own foreign policy.
Washington's Proclamation of Neutrality declared the United States would take neither side, and the precedent that the president leads in foreign affairs largely dates from this unilateral act.

Jay's Treaty got the British forts evacuated and avoided a war the country could not afford but conceded almost everything else and won nothing on impressment, triggering the most violent partisan eruption of the era.
Pinckney's Treaty with a distracted Spain guaranteed American farmers the right to ship crops down the Mississippi through New Orleans, the lifeline of the western economy.

In 1794 western Pennsylvania erupted in the Whiskey Rebellion against the excise tax, and Washington federalized around thirteen thousand militia and rode west, the rebellion evaporating without a battle.
Where the Confederation had been helpless against Shays, the new government could enforce a law by force and then show mercy, the Constitution's answer that the republic could keep order.

Washington's 1796 Farewell Address, released to a newspaper rather than delivered aloud, warned against the spirit of party and permanent alliances, shaping American foreign policy for over a century.

title: The Adams Interlude
bullets: John Adams won the election of 1796 narrowly over Jefferson, and by a quirk of the original Constitution the new president was a Federalist and his vice president the leader of the opposition party.
The Quasi-War was an undeclared naval conflict with France, which began seizing American merchant ships in large numbers after the United States signed Jay's Treaty.
In the XYZ Affair, French intermediaries demanded a bribe and a loan before talks could begin, and 'Millions for defense, but not one cent for tribute' swept the country.
The 1798 Sedition Act made it a federal crime to write or speak 'false, scandalous, and malicious' statements against the government, and it was used to jail Republican editors and a congressman.

The Kentucky and Virginia Resolutions, secretly written by Jefferson and Madison, asserted that a state may declare a federal law void, the doctrine of nullification, drawn first in the cause of free speech.
Britain had passed almost identical wartime gag laws in 1795, so the model of liberty the founders kept invoking was criminalizing dissent at the same moment America was.
In 1799 Adams sent a new peace commission to Paris over his own party's open opposition, ending the Quasi-War by negotiation and preventing a war that would have been wildly popular.
That choice destroyed Adams politically, split the Federalists, and like Washington's resignation was a quiet load-bearing act: a president who could have ridden a war to power chose not to.

title: The Revolution of 1800
bullets: The election of 1800 is the most important event in this book for something that did not happen: no army marched, no one was executed, and a party that controlled everything handed over power after losing.
A design flaw let Jefferson and his running mate Aaron Burr tie with identical electoral votes, throwing the election into a House still controlled by defeated Federalists.
For thirty-five ballots the House deadlocked while some Federalists schemed to install Burr, until Hamilton's private campaign by letter against Burr helped Jefferson win on the thirty-sixth ballot.
Measured against all of recorded history, power passing peacefully to people who had been calling the incumbents traitors was close to a first in the modern world, and it was not guaranteed.
In his 1801 inaugural Jefferson said 'We are all republicans, we are all federalists,' the founding statement of the loyal opposition, that the loser need not fear the winner.
The Twelfth Amendment in 1804 required electors to cast separate labeled votes for president and vice president, patching the structural bug that nearly crashed the machine.
The defeated Federalists planted midnight judges, and one undelivered commission for William Marbury produced Marbury v. Madison, which trapped the new Chief Justice John Marshall.

Marshall claimed an enormous power, judicial review, in the act of declining a small one, establishing that when a law conflicts with the Constitution the Constitution wins, a near-unique idea at the time.

title: The Jeffersonian Experiment
bullets: Jefferson governed at first exactly as advertised, walking to his own inauguration, slashing the budget, shrinking the army and navy, letting the Sedition Act expire, and repealing the whiskey excise.
The Louisiana Purchase broke the theory open: Napoleon, having lost a brutal war in Saint-Domingue where the enslaved won independence as Haiti, abruptly offered to sell the entire territory.
For roughly fifteen million dollars the United States bought about 828,000 square miles, doubling its national size overnight in a single transaction without firing a shot.

Jefferson believed his own strict-construction principles required a constitutional amendment but swallowed his doctrine and justified the purchase under the treaty power, performing the largest loose-construction act in the country's history.
Lewis and Clark went up the Missouri, over the Rockies, to the Pacific and back, surviving in part because of the guidance and diplomacy of a teenaged Shoshone woman, Sacagawea.
The Purchase doubled the land slavery could grow on while Britain's abolitionists were banning the slave trade in 1807, the two English-speaking powers moving in opposite directions.
Jefferson is the human form of the book's contradiction: he wrote that all men are created equal and enslaved more than six hundred people, freeing almost none.

A 1998 DNA study plus the documentary record led the institution running Monticello and most historians to conclude Jefferson fathered the children of the enslaved Sally Hemings, a story long dismissed as slander.

title: Cotton and the Second Slavery
bullets: In 1790 a reasonable observer could believe slavery was slowly dying; by 1820 it was the fastest-growing and most profitable institution in the country, expanding across half a continent.
In 1793 Eli Whitney built the cotton gin, a simple drum with wire teeth that separated fiber from sticky seed dozens of times faster than a hand, making short-staple cotton enormously profitable.

The mechanized textile mills of the British Industrial Revolution devoured raw cotton faster than the world could grow it, so British spinning and the American gin were one event with an ocean in the middle.
A near-infinite demand for enslaved labor made slavery the most valuable economic engine in the country, a new modern system historians call the Second Slavery.

After the Atlantic trade closed, the domestic slave trade moved on the order of a million enslaved people from the Upper South to the cotton frontier, marched in coffles with families broken up by sale.
The same world economy was destroying millions of Indian handloom weavers, so the Mississippi plantation, the Manchester factory, and the ruined Indian weaver were three points on one global circuit.

Congress banned the importation of enslaved people from Africa effective January 1, 1808, but because the enslaved population grew by natural increase, it functioned like a protective tariff on American-born enslaved people.
By 1820 the single most dynamic sector of the American economy was slave-grown cotton for the world's factories, the institution getting larger, richer, and more entrenched every year.

title: Faith, Mills, and Roads
bullets: Three transformations between 1790 and 1820 turned a loose collection of regions into a society, while pulling the country together along an east-west axis that left the slave South on the other side of a widening seam.
The Second Great Awakening swept the country, and at Cane Ridge in Kentucky in 1801 a crowd estimated at ten to twenty thousand fell, wept, and shook in mass emotional conversion.

The revival shifted American Protestantism from Calvinist predestination toward the idea anyone could choose salvation, and its social fuse was reform: temperance, public education, prison reform, and abolitionism.

Richard Allen, pulled from his knees and ordered to a segregated gallery while praying, walked out and in 1816 founded the African Methodist Episcopal Church, the first independent Black denomination.

American religion became entirely voluntary as the states disestablished their official churches one by one, a historical novelty that made religion more energetic, not less, contrary to European expectation.

Samuel Slater slipped out of England disguised as a farm laborer with the spinning machinery memorized in his head and rebuilt it in Rhode Island in 1790, so American industrialization began as espionage.
Robert Fulton ran the first commercially successful steamboat up the Hudson in 1807, and in 1817 New York began the Erie Canal, cutting shipping costs to the interior by roughly ninety percent.

The factories and canals ran predominantly east-west through the free states rather than across the slavery line, so the forces knitting the nation together also drew the seam along which it would one day tear.

title: The Road to War
bullets: The Napoleonic Wars became a death struggle between Napoleon's France, dominating the land, and Britain, dominating the sea, and a neutral commercial nation got crushed in between.

Britain practiced impressment, stopping American ships and seizing sailors it claimed were British subjects; thousands of genuine American citizens were taken because the British Navy was desperate for men.
The 1807 Chesapeake-Leopard affair, a British attack on a United States Navy vessel in peacetime, erupted into fury Jefferson could have turned into a war with popular support.
Jefferson instead chose the Embargo of 1807, banning American ships from foreign ports, which devastated Americans far more than Britain and left grass growing on idle wharves before being repealed.
After the 1810 elections the War Hawks, led by the Kentuckian Henry Clay, argued submission was destroying national honor and that war might let the United States seize Canada.
American expansion pressed into Native lands through structurally fraudulent treaties signed by a few cooperative chiefs who, by their own peoples' rules, had no right to cede the land.

The Shawnee leader Tecumseh and his brother the Prophet built a pan-Indian confederacy at Prophetstown on the radical argument that no single chief could sell land held by all Native peoples in common.

In November 1811 William Henry Harrison fought the Prophet's followers at Tippecanoe and burned the town, fusing the frontier war and maritime grievances, and in June 1812 a divided Congress declared war by an unusually close vote.

title: The War of 1812
bullets: The United States entered the war divided, fought it mostly badly, did not win, and emerged convinced it had triumphed, a war whose most famous victory was fought after peace had been signed.
The opening strategy to conquer Canada was a fiasco: bungled invasions collapsed and an American army surrendered Detroit without much of a fight.
In August 1814 a British force scattered American militia at Bladensburg, marched into the capital, ate the dinner laid out in the President's House, and burned it and the Capitol.
Oliver Hazard Perry won the Battle of Lake Erie in 1813, reporting it in the line 'We have met the enemy and they are ours,' which let Harrison win the Battle of the Thames where Tecumseh was killed.
Tecumseh's death shattered the pan-Indian confederacy and ended the prospect of an organized Native barrier to American expansion east of the Mississippi, the war's most consequential result.
Andrew Jackson crushed the Red Sticks at Horseshoe Bend in 1814 and imposed a treaty stripping the Creeks of a vast territory, opening the future cotton lands of Alabama and Mississippi.

The Treaty of Ghent ended the war on the basis of status quo ante bellum, with no territory changed and impressment not even mentioned, yet 'nothing changed' changed everything in American self-perception.

Two weeks after the treaty, Jackson's fortified position shattered a British army at New Orleans, manufacturing a national hero, while the secret Hartford Convention's timing made New England Federalists look like traitors and killed their party.

title: The Era of Good Feelings
bullets: For about a decade after the war the United States had only one political party, and James Monroe, the last of the Virginia founders, won the presidency in 1816 and 1820 essentially unopposed.

The surviving party adopted much of the dead party's program, turning to economic nationalism because the War of 1812 had nearly been lost without a national bank, protected industry, or decent roads.
Henry Clay packaged the American System, a closed loop in which a protective tariff shields Northern industry and raises money, a national bank steadies credit, and the money builds roads and canals.
The Second Bank of the United States was chartered in 1816 because the same Jeffersonian Republicans who had let the first Bank die in 1811 reversed themselves after the war's financial catastrophe.

In McCulloch v. Maryland in 1819, Marshall held Congress could charter a bank under implied powers and that 'the power to tax involves the power to destroy,' so no state could tax a federal instrument.
In 1823 the Monroe Doctrine declared the American continents closed to new European colonization, a bold statement riding on the British Navy's power since the United States could not enforce it.
The Panic of 1819 was the young republic's first great boom-and-bust crash, foreclosing farms by the thousands and leaving a deep popular suspicion of banks that Jackson would later weaponize.
The good feelings were a deceptive surface over a country indebted, sectionally divided, and about to be asked the one question it had spent thirty years refusing to answer.

title: The Fire Bell and the Founders' Exit
bullets: In 1819 the territory of Missouri applied for statehood as a slave state, and a New York congressman's amendment to end slavery there passed the House on a stark sectional vote.
The country suddenly saw its deepest fault line ran not between parties but between North and South along the line of slavery, and the constitutional machinery offered no automatic answer.
The Missouri Compromise of 1820, engineered by Henry Clay, admitted Missouri as a slave state, added Maine as a free state, and forbade slavery north of latitude 36°30' in the rest of Louisiana.
Britain in the same years was abolishing slavery in its empire outright, which it did in 1833, so one Atlantic power divided a continent to preserve the institution while the other ended it.
Jefferson, reading the news in retirement, wrote that the controversy 'like a fire bell in the night, awakened and filled me with terror,' calling it the knell of the Union.

The 1824 election split four Republicans; Jackson won the most votes but not a majority, and when Clay threw his support to Adams, who then made Clay Secretary of State, Jackson's supporters called it the corrupt bargain.
On July 4, 1826, the fiftieth anniversary of the Declaration, Thomas Jefferson and John Adams both died within hours of each other, Adams's last words being that 'Thomas Jefferson survives.'
The election of 1828 swept Andrew Jackson into the presidency on a broadened white-male franchise, the threshold of mass democracy, with the slavery contradiction now armed rather than fading.
